title: Behavioral Finance and Cognitive Biases
subtitle: Endowment effect, herd behavior, sunk cost fallacy
description: This checklist reveals how psychology and evolutionary mental errors influence our financial decisions. You'll learn about key cognitive biases that cause us to buy high, sell low, hold losing assets, and follow the crowd. Understanding these traps is the first step toward rational money management.

items.title: Loss Aversion
items.source: Daniel Kahneman & Amos Tversky — Prospect Theory
items.text: A fundamental discovery of behavioral economics: the pain of losing 1000 rubles is 2-2.5 times stronger than the pleasure of finding 1000 rubles. In practice, this leads investors to hold falling stocks too long (unwilling to lock in a loss) and sell rising stocks too soon (rushing to lock in a profit). The result: a portfolio filled with 'toxic' assets, missing growth potential.
items.tip: Establish a rule: pre-define stop-loss (e.g., -15%) and take-profit (e.g., +30%) levels when buying any asset. Follow the rule, not your emotions.

items.title: Endowment Effect
items.text: We tend to overvalue what we already own, simply because it's 'ours.' In a classic experiment, students given a free mug were willing to sell it for an average of $7, while those without the mug were willing to buy it for only $3. In investing, this manifests as reluctance to sell inherited stocks, inherited apartment at market price, or to part with 'favorite' but losing stocks.
items.tip: Once a quarter, ask about each asset: 'Would I buy this today at its current price if I didn't own it?' If the answer is 'no' — sell.

items.title: Herd Behavior
items.text: Evolutionarily, following the crowd was a survival strategy: if everyone runs from a predator, it's better to run with them even if you don't see the danger. In financial markets, this mechanism leads to bubbles (everyone buys, I buy at the peak) and panics (everyone sells, I sell at the bottom). Tulipomania, the dot-com bubble, and crypto-hype are classic examples of herd behavior.
items.tip: When everyone around is talking about one asset (a taxi driver recommends buying stocks), it's a danger signal. Ask: 'Who is left? Who hasn't bought yet?' If the answer is 'nobody,' the bubble is near its peak.

items.title: Sunk Cost Fallacy
items.source: Hal Arkes — The Psychology of Sunk Cost
items.text: We tend to continue investing resources (money, time, effort) in a losing venture just because we've already invested a lot. 'I paid for a gym membership, I have to go even if I hate it.' 'I'm holding these stocks because they've already dropped 50%, waiting to get back to purchase price.' Rationally, sunk costs shouldn't affect future decisions. Only the future balance of benefits and costs matters.
items.tip: When deciding to continue a project or hold an asset, mentally 'zero out' all past investments. Ask: 'If I were starting from scratch today, would I invest in this?'

items.title: Confirmation Bias
items.text: We seek and interpret information to confirm our existing beliefs, ignoring contradictory data. An investor who bought a company's stock will read only positive reports and news, ignoring signals of trouble. This leads to overconfidence and holding bad assets until complete collapse.
items.tip: Consciously seek information contradicting your investment thesis. Appoint a 'devil's advocate' in your thinking who will prove why you are wrong.

items.title: Illusion of Control
items.source: Ellen Langer — The Illusion of Control
items.text: People overestimate their ability to control random events. Dice players throw harder when wanting high numbers. Investors believe their analysis or intuition can predict market movements, though thousands of studies prove active portfolio management underperforms passive index investing in the long run.
items.tip: Separate what you can control (diversification, costs, taxes, behavior) from what you cannot (market movement, central bank rate, news).

items.title: Availability Heuristic
items.text: We assess event probability by how easily we can recall examples. After a plane crash, people fear flying, though statistically it's the safest transport. After news of a crypto-hack, all exchanges seem unreliable. After a sharp rise in a stock, it seems it will rise forever. Vivid, emotional events distort our perception of probabilities.
items.tip: When making a financial decision, ask: 'Do I have statistical data on this, or am I relying on the last vivid news?'

items.title: Temporal Discounting
items.text: We overvalue immediate rewards and undervalue future benefits. 'I'll buy this gadget now, save for retirement later.' This hyperbolic discounting explains why people don't save enough for retirement, take expensive loans, and impulsively spend bonuses. Our brain is evolutionarily wired for survival now, not planning 30 years ahead.
items.tip: Automate savings: set up an automatic transfer of 10-20% of income to a separate account on payday. Remove 'friction' for correct behavior.

items.title: Mental Accounting
items.text: We treat money differently depending on its source and intended purpose. Bonuses or winnings are spent more easily than salary. 'Rainy day' funds are untouched even if there is an expensive loan (though rationally, paying off the loan saves interest). Investors often risk 'profits' (house money) but are afraid to touch the 'deposit principal.' From an economic perspective, all money is the same.
items.tip: Treat all money as a single capital pool. Don't create separate mental 'wallets' with different 'spending rules.'

items.title: Hindsight Bias
items.text: After an event occurs, it seems we 'knew it all along.' 'I told you the market would fall!' (though took no action before the fall). This illusion of past predictability leads to overconfidence in future forecasts and excessive risk-taking. It prevents learning from actual mistakes, as the brain rewrites history to make us appear smarter than we were.
items.tip: Keep an investment diary. Record your forecast and reasoning BEFORE you know the outcome. Compare after a month/year.

quiz.q: What is 'loss aversion' in Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory?
a: The pain of loss is 2-2.5 times stronger than the pleasure of gain

quiz.q: What is the essence of the 'sunk cost fallacy'?
a: Continuing to invest in a losing venture due to resources already spent

quiz.q: How does the 'endowment effect' influence investment decisions?
a: Leads to overvaluing what we already own

quiz.q: What is 'herd behavior' in financial markets?
a: The tendency to follow the actions of the crowd

quiz.q: How to fight 'confirmation bias' when analyzing investments?
a: Consciously seek information contradicting your opinion
